body_txt: Classics, almost by definition, are works that are considered to be of high quality, are influential on later works, and are widely known. However, one will often find that only scholars and enthusiasts have first-hand knowledge of the material in question, and that the masses know it either only by title or by homages, parodies, direct references and allusions found in more populist works. Essentially, various bits and pieces of high culture are most widely known through their use in pop culture (which also puts them on track to become standard snippets ). Ill-informed people might even think these bits and pieces are original to the popular work, And That's Terrible ( nine times out of ten ). Stock Shout-Outs such as Pietà Plagiarism exist because of this phenomenon. (Most artists would be copying some other usage than the sculpture.) Frequently results in Beam Me Up, Scotty! , It Was His Sled , and Covered Up . See also Half-Remembered Homage . See also these tropes:  Compare/Contrast Pop-Cultural Osmosis Failure (when someone doesn't get the reference), Pop-Culture Isolation (in which the reference is well-known in one or a few major areas, but virtually unknown elsewhere).
